description: This test case evaluates the system's ability to retrieve and synthesize information from multiple, separate document sections to answer a complex question.
question: Explain how the choice of silicon wafer orientation influences the hole mobility and Negative Bias Temperature Instability (NBTI) degradation in p-MOSFET devices, and discuss the implications of this on three-dimensional device structures such as FinFETs fabricated on (100) wafers. Additionally, analyze the measurement challenges associated with characterizing NBTI degradation and how modern techniques address these challenges.

gold_context_sources.evidence: Most ICs are fabricated on (100) oriented Si wafers. However, it has been known since 1968 that hole mobility is higher for p-MOSFETs on (110) oriented wafers with the channel in the (110) direction [39,40]. Along with the higher hole mobility, however, is a higher interface trap density. Dy has been reported as D;(100)=7.7x 10" and Dy(110)=14x10"cm2eV™' [41]. Hence one would expect more severe NBTI degradation on (110) oriented wafers, as indeed has been observed, as shown in Fig. 11 [42,43]. This is a potential problem if (1 10) oriented wafers become important. For certain three-dimensional devices, e.g., FInFETs, when fabricated on (100) wafers with the channel in the conventional (110) direction, the vertical sidewalls are (110) oriented, leading to NBTI problems [42]. However, forming FinFETs on (100) wafers with the channel in the (100) direction, leads to (100) ver- tical sidewalls, illustrated in Fig. 12.

Traditionally NBTI has been characterized by stressing a device, then interrupting the stress to measure a device parameter, e.g., VT, ID, leﬀ, Dit, Not, stress again, measure, etc. VT, ID, and leﬀ are determined from ID–VD and ID–VG measurements. The interface trap density Dit is usually measured with charge pumping [45] or log(ID)–VG sub- threshold swing measurements. Usually source, drain and substrate are grounded during the stress period. The ‘‘dead’’ time between ‘‘stress’’ and ‘‘measure’’ varied from researcher to researcher and was frequently not given in publications. When it was discovered that that time was quite important, techniques were developed to minimize and eliminate the ‘‘dead’’ time. Rangan et al. use VD = 50 mV and monitor the drain current during stress and recovery [22]. Drain current depends on eﬀective mobility and threshold voltage and in this method neither is measured explicitly, but the drain current degradation is determined. Huard et al. in their “on the fly”” method, apply a low drain voltage pulse of ~50 mV and record the drain current at the end of the stress period, without interrupting the stress, illustrated in Fig. 13(a) [4]. This was repeated and in this way drain current degradation was recorded with no “dead” time. Since the current depends on threshold voltage, mobility and other device parameters, it is not possible to extract DVT.

ground_truth_answer:
The orientation of silicon wafers significantly affects the electrical characteristics of p-MOSFET devices, particularly hole mobility and susceptibility to Negative Bias Temperature Instability (NBTI) degradation. ICs are predominantly fabricated on (100) oriented silicon wafers. However, it has been established since 1968 that p-MOSFETs fabricated on (110) oriented wafers with the channel aligned in the (110) direction exhibit higher hole mobility compared to those on (100) wafers. This increase in hole mobility is beneficial for device performance. Nevertheless, this advantage comes at the cost of a higher interface trap density (Dy), reported as approximately 7.7 x 10^?? cm^2 eV^-1 for (100) and roughly double at 14 x 10^?? cm^2 eV^-1 for (110) wafers. The elevated trap density on (110) wafers leads to more severe NBTI degradation, a reliability concern characterized by threshold voltage shifts and mobility degradation under negative bias and temperature stress. Such degradation has been experimentally observed and documented, indicating that the higher hole mobility advantage is tempered by worsened reliability on (110) orientations.

In the context of three-dimensional devices such as FinFETs, wafer orientation plays a crucial role in determining sidewall crystallography and thus NBTI behavior. FinFETs fabricated on (100) wafers with the channel in the conventional (110) direction have vertical sidewalls oriented in the (110) plane, which subjects these surfaces to the higher interface trap density and consequent NBTI degradation similar to planar devices on (110) wafers. To mitigate this, forming FinFETs on (100) wafers with the channel aligned along the (100) direction results in (100) oriented vertical sidewalls, which benefit from lower interface trap densities and improved NBTI reliability.

Characterizing NBTI degradation poses measurement challenges, primarily due to the conventional approach involving interrupting stress to measure device parameters like threshold voltage (VT), drain current (ID), effective channel length (leff), interface trap density (Dit), and trapped charge density (Not). Interruptions introduce a 'dead' time between stress and measurement, during which some recovery of the device may occur, leading to inaccurate or non-representative degradation assessment. Early studies often neglected this dead time or varied it without standardization.

Modern measurement techniques have been developed to minimize or eliminate dead time and provide more accurate real-time monitoring of NBTI degradation. For example, Rangan et al. applied a low drain voltage (VD = 50 mV) and monitored the drain current continuously during stress and recovery without interrupting the stress. Although this method measures drain current degradation influenced by threshold voltage and mobility changes, it does not explicitly extract VT shifts. Huard et al. advanced this approach by applying low drain voltage pulses (~50 mV) and measuring the drain current at the end of the stress period without interrupting stress, termed the “on-the-fly” method. This technique effectively eliminates dead time and captures degradation continuously. Furthermore, modifications involving superimposed small gate voltage pulses allow extraction of transconductance, providing more insight into device parameter changes during NBTI stress. These advanced techniques enhance the understanding of NBTI by providing continuous, real-time data, circumventing previous measurement limitations associated with dead time and recovery effects.
